ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated entry for laksa leaf as a culinary herb; closest defensible match is Vietnamese coriander plant material under FOODON_03301710. Verified as Persicaria odorata.
ontology.foodex2_description: Herbs and spices — fresh herbs (FoodEx2 level 3 category anchor for fresh culinary herbs)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No species-specific FoodEx2 code exists for Persicaria odorata (laksa leaf). Mapped to the fresh herbs category anchor A00LC as the closest defensible FoodEx2 classification.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept found for laksa leaf / Vietnamese coriander as a food item. SNOMED CT coverage for culinary herbs is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical garnish serving of fresh laksa leaf is approximately 5 g (about 1 tablespoon of loosely packed leaves), as used in a bowl of laksa or Peranakan dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Laksa Leaf (Polygonum odoratum) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for laksa leaf (Polygonum odoratum / Persicaria odorata), confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input record. All per-100g values preserved from the HPB source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimation for low-carbohydrate culinary herbs — analogous estimate based on non-starchy leafy herb category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous category reference)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for laksa leaf (Persicaria odorata) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Laksa leaf contains only 0.97 g carbohydrate per 100 g. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) by analogy with other non-starchy culinary herbs and leafy greens in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently fall in the low GI range (GI < 20) due to negligible available carbohydrate. Glycemic load per 5 g serving = (15 × 0.97 × 0.05) / 100 ≈ 0.01, which is nutritionally negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (23 mg/100 g) and high potassium (359 mg/100 g) yield a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Laksa leaf is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its very low sodium content (23 mg/100 g) and high potassium (359 mg/100 g) actively support blood pressure reduction, and its high dietary fibre (9 g/100 g) contributes to reduced arterial stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely low available carbohydrate (0.97 g/100 g) and very high dietary fibre (9 g/100 g) result in negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Laksa leaf is highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its near-zero available carbohydrate (0.97 g/100 g) and very high dietary fibre (9 g/100 g) produce a negligible glycaemic response, and it can be used freely as a herb garnish without affecting postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.44 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (9 g/100 g), high potassium (359 mg/100 g), and high iron (5.59 mg/100 g) support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Laksa leaf is suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; its very low total fat, high dietary fibre supporting LDL reduction, and high potassium supporting vascular tone make it a cardioprotective culinary herb when used as a garnish or flavouring.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols, high calcium (191 mg/100 g) beneficial for patients on corticosteroids, and high dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome immune regulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Laksa leaf is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its established anti-inflammatory properties, high calcium content (191 mg/100 g) supporting bone health in corticosteroid-treated patients, and high dietary fibre promoting gut microbiome diversity make it a beneficial culinary herb.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Laksa leaf (Persicaria odorata), also known as Vietnamese coriander, Vietnamese mint, or daun kesum in Malay, is a pungent aromatic culinary herb native to Southeast Asia and widely used in Singaporean, Peranakan, Malaysian, and Vietnamese cuisines. The leaves are dark green, pointed, and tender, with a distinctive peppery, citrus-like flavour that is sharper and more assertive than common coriander. In Singapore, laksa leaf is the defining garnish of laksa — a rich, spicy coconut-milk noodle soup — and is also used in Peranakan dishes, rojak, and various hawker preparations. The herb is used raw, added at the point of serving to preserve its volatile aromatic compounds. Nutritionally, laksa leaf is exceptionally low in calories (40 kcal/100 g) and available carbohydrates (0.97 g/100 g), while being remarkably high in dietary fibre (9 g/100 g), protein for a herb (3.47 g/100 g), calcium (191 mg/100 g), iron (5.59 mg/100 g), and potassium (359 mg/100 g). Its very low sodium (23 mg/100 g) and high potassium make it ideal for hypertension management under DASH dietary principles. The negligible carbohydrate content makes it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and those following ketogenic or low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The herb's polyphenol and antioxidant content, combined with its high fibre, supports gut microbiome health and anti-inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease prevention. Laksa leaf is classified as a raw, minimally processed fresh herb and is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is laksa leaf the same as Vietnamese mint?
What does daun kesum taste like?
Is laksa leaf good for diabetes?
Can I eat laksa leaf if I have high blood pressure?
What herb is used in laksa soup?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the canonical input record. The food is a well-documented culinary herb with an exact match in the HPB database. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence for GI only) due to absence of direct GI measurement for this herb, but the estimate is defensible given the negligible carbohydrate content.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, sugar_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, sugar breakdown, micronutrient panel beyond calcium, iron, and potassium are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for laksa leaf as of 2026-04-16. 2. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database category for non-starchy culinary herbs and leafy greens; no direct GI measurement for Persicaria odorata exists in peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per 5 g serving is nutritionally negligible at 0.01. 3. The input record assigned a GI of 66 (medium) from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this has been rejected as the source is not an approved GI source per Project Veritas v2 policy, and a GI of 66 is implausible for a food with 0.97 g/100 g carbohydrate. 4. The input dietary_tag 'wholegrain' has been removed as laksa leaf is a fresh herb, not a grain product. 5. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 is used as an approximate match; no exact species-level FoodOn entry for Persicaria odorata was confirmed. 6. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as a category-level anchor for fresh herbs; no species-specific FoodEx2 code for laksa leaf was confirmed.
